## SELF-SUPERVISED VARIATIONAL AUTO-ENCODERS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

intelligence. Variational Auto-Encoders (VAEs) constitute a single framework to achieve these goals. Here, we present a novel class of generative models, called _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoder_ (selfVAE), that utilizes deterministic and discrete transformations of data. This class of models allows performing both conditional and unconditional sampling while simplifying the objective function. First, we use a single self-supervised transformation as a latent variable, where a transformation is either downscaling or edge detection. Next, we consider a hierarchical architecture, i.e., multiple transformations, and we show its benefits compared to the VAE. The flexibility of selfVAE in data reconstruction finds a particularly interesting use case in data compression tasks, where we can trade-off memory for better data quality, and vice-versa. We present the performance of our approach on three benchmark image data (Cifar10, Imagenette64, and CelebA).

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The framework of variational autoencoders (VAEs) provides a principled approach for learning latentvariable models. As it utilizes a meaningful low-dimensional latent space with density estimation capabilities, it forms an attractive solution for generative modelling tasks. However, its performance in terms of the test log-likelihood and quality of generated samples is often disappointing, thus, many modifications were proposed. In general, one can obtain a tighter lower bound, and, thus, a more powerful and flexible model, by advancing over the following three components: the _encoder_ (Rezende et al., 2014; van den Berg et al., 2018; Hoogeboom et al., 2020; Maaløe et al., 2016), the _prior_ (or _marginal_ over latents) (Chen et al., 2016; Habibian et al., 2019; Lavda et al., 2020; Lin & Clark, 2020; Tomczak & Welling, 2017) and the _decoder_ (Gulrajani et al., 2016). Recent studies have shown that by employing deep hierarchical architectures and by carefully designing building blocks of the neural networks, VAEs can successfully model high-dimensional data and reach state-of-the-art test likelihoods (Zhao et al., 2017; Maaløe et al., 2019; Vahdat & Kautz, 2020).

In this work, we present a novel class of VAEs, called _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders_ , where we introduce additional variables to VAEs that result from discrete and deterministic transformations of observed images. Since the transformations are deterministic, and they provide a specific aspect of images (e.g., contextual information through detecting edges or downscaling), we refer to them as _self-supervised representations_ . The introduction of the discrete and deterministic variables allows to train deep hierarchical models efficiently by decomposing the task of learning a highly complex distribution into training smaller and conditional distributions. In this way, the model allows to integrate the prior knowledge about the data, but still enables to synthesize unconditional samples. Furthermore, the discrete and deterministic variables could be used to conditionally reconstruct data, which could be of great use in data compression and super-resolution tasks.

We make the following contributions: i) We propose an extension of the VAE framework by incorporating self-supervised representations of the data. ii) We analyze the impact of modelling natural images with different data transformations as self-supervised representations. iii) This new type of generative model ( _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders_ ), which is able to perform both conditional and unconditional sampling, demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation and generative capabilities on image benchmarks.

The expectation is approximated by Monte Carlo sampling while exploiting the _reparameterization trick_ in order to obtain unbiased gradient estimators. The models are parameterized by neural networks. This generative framework is known as _Variational Auto-Encoder_ (VAE) (Kingma & Welling, 2013; Rezende et al., 2014).

## 2.2 VAES WITH BIJECTIVE PRIORS

Even though the lower-bound suggests that the prior plays a crucial role in improving the variational bounds, usually a fixed distribution is used, e.g., a standard multivariate Gaussian. While being relatively simple and computationally cheap, the fixed prior is known to result in over-regularized models that tend to ignore most of the latent dimensions (Burda et al., 2015; Hoffman & Johnson, 2016; Tomczak & Welling, 2017). Moreover, even with powerful encoders, VAEs may still fail to match the variational posterior to a unit Gaussian prior (Rosca et al., 2018).

However, it is possible to obtain a rich, multi-modal prior distribution _p_ ( **z** ) by using a _bijective_ (or _flow-based_ ) model (Dinh et al., 2016). ## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 MOTIVATION

The idea of self-supervised learning is about utilizing original unlabeled data to create additional context information. It could be achieved in multiple manners, e.g., by adding noise to data (Vincent et al., 2008) or masking data during training (Zhang et al., 2017). Self-supervised learning could also be seen as turning an unsupervised model into a supervised by, e.g., treating predicting next pixels as a classification task (Hénaff et al., 2019; Oord et al., 2018). These are only a few examples of a quickly growing research line (Liu et al., 2020).

Here, we propose to use non-trainable transformations to obtain information about image data. Our main hypothesis is that since working with highly-quality images is challenging, we could alleviate this problem by additionally considering partial information about them. Fitting a model to images of lower quality, and then enhancing them to match the target distribution seems to be overall an easier task (Chang et al., 2004; Gatopoulos et al., 2020). By incorporating compressed transformations (i.e., the self-supervised representations) that still contain global information, with the premise that it would be easier to approximate, the process of modelling a high-dimensional complex density breaks down into simpler tasks. In this way, the expressivity of the model will grow and gradually result into richer, better generations. A positive effect of the proposed framework is that the model allows us to integrate prior knowledge through the image transformations, without losing its uncon-

## 3.2 MODEL FORMULATION

In our model, we consider representations that result from _deterministic_ and _discrete_ transformations of an image. Formally, we introduce a transformation _d_ : _X[D] ! X[C]_ that takes **x** and returns an image representation **y** , e.g., a downscaled image. Since we lose information about the original image, **z** could be seen as a variable that compensates lost details in **x** . Further we propose to introduce an additional latent variable, **u** _2_ R _[N]_ to model **y** and **z** . We can define the joint distribution of **x** and **y** as follows: _p_ ( **x** _,_ **y** ) = _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) _p_ ( **x** ), where _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) = _δ_ ( **y** _−d_ ( **x** )) due to the deterministic transformation _d_ ( _·_ ), where _δ_ ( _·_ ) is the Kronecker delta. Thus, the empirical distribution is _δ_ ( **y** _− d_ ( **x** )) _pdata_ ( **x** ). Intuitively, the premise for selfVAE is that the latents **u** will capture the global structure of the input data and the latents **z** will encode the missing information between **y** and **x** , guiding the model to discover the distribution of the target observations. In order to highlight the self-supervised part in our model, we refer to it as the _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoder_ (or selfVAE for short).

Notice that we use the discretized logistic distribution, because images are represented by values between 0 and 255. For integer-valued random variables, other distributions like Gaussian are inappropriate.

The presented versions of generating and reconstructing images could be useful in the compression task. However, every inferred variable needs to be sent, thus, more sampling corresponds to lower memory requirements.

## 3.4 HIERARCHICAL SELF-SUPERVISED VAE

The proposed approach can be further extended and generalized by introducing multiple transformations, in the way that it is illustrated in Figure 3. By incorporating a single (or multiple) self-supervised representation(s) of the data, the process of modelling a high-dimensional complex density breaks down into _K_ simpler modeling tasks. Thus, we obtain a _K_ -level VAE architecture, where the overall expressivity of the model grows even further and gradually results into generations of higher quality. Some transformations cannot be applied multiple times (e.g., edge detection), however, others could be used sequentially, e.g., downscaling.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

**Datasets** We evaluate the proposed model on CIFAR-10, Imagenette64 and CelebA:

_CIFAR-10_ The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set. We augment the training data by using random horizontal flips and random affine transformations and normalize the data uniformly in the range (0, 1).

_Imagenette64_ Imagenette64[2] is a subset of 10 classes from the downscaled Imagenet dataset. We downscaled the dataset to 64px _⇥_ 64px images. Similarly to CIFAR-10, we put aside 15% randomly selected training images as the test set. We used the same data augmentation as in CIFAR-10

_CelebA_ The Large-scale CelebFaces Attributes (CelebA) Dataset consists of 202 _._ 599 images of celebrities. We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the top left corner of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148. Besides the uniform normalization of the image, no other augmentation was applied.

**Architectures** Encoders and decoders consist of building blocks composed of DenseNets (Huang et al., 2016), channel-wise attention (Zhang et al., 2018), and ELUs (Clevert et al., 2015) as activation functions. The dimensionality of all the latent variables were kept at 8 _⇥_ 8 _⇥_ 16 = 1024 and all models were trained using AdaMax (Kingma & Ba, 2014) with data-dependent initialization (Salimans & Kingma, 2016). Regarding the selfVAEs, in CIFAR-10 we used an architecture with a single downscaled transformation (selfVAE-downscale), while on the remaining two datasets (CelebA

## 4.2 QUANTITATIVE RESULTS

We present the results of the experiments on the benchmark datasets in Table 1. First, we notice that on CIFAR-10 our implementation of the VAE is still lacking behind other generative models in terms of _bpd_ , however, it is better or comparable in terms of FID. The selfVAE-downscale achieves worse _bpd_ than the VAE. A possible explanation may lie in the small image size (32 _⇥_ 32), as the benefits of breaking down the learning process in two or more steps are not obvious given the small target dimensional space. Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models. This result could follow from the fact that downscaling allows to maintain context information about the original image and, as a result, a general coherence is of higher quality.

_bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit of employing a multileveled self-supervised framework against the VAE in higher-dimensional data, where the plain model fails to scale efficiently. It seems that the hierarchical structure of self-supervised random variables allows to encode the missing information more efficiently in **z** _k_ , in contrast to the vanilla VAE, where all information about images must be coded in **z** . This result is promising and indicates that the proposed approach is of great potential for generative modelling.

from VAE that produces overall more contextless and distorted generations. This result seems to be in line with the FID scores. Especially for CelebA, we observe impressive synthesis quality, great sampling diversity and coherent generations (Figure 5). On the Imagenette64 dataset, we can also observe crisper generations for our method compared to the VAE (Figure 4).

Furthermore, the hierarchical selfVAE seems to be of a great potential for compression purposes. In contrast to the VAE, which is restricted to using a single way of reconstructing an image, the selfVAE allows four various options with different quality/memory ratios (Figure 6). In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to **z** (Figure 11). This shows the advantage of choosing prior knowledge into the learning process. Lastly, the latents learn to add extra information, which defines the end result, and we can alter details of an image like facial expressions (Figure 12.ii).

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we showed that taking deterministic and discrete transformations results in coherent generations of high visual quality, and allows to integrate prior knowledge without loosing its unconditional generative functionality. The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better _bpd_ scores and better generations and reconstructions. In the experiments, we considered two classes of image transformations, namely, _downscaling_ and edge detection ( _sketching_ ). However, there is a vast of possible other transformations (see Figure 2), and we leave investigating them for future work. Moreover, we find the proposed approach interesting for the compression task. A similar approach with a multi-scale auto-encoder for image compression was proposed, e.g, by Mentzer et al. (2019) or Razavi et al. (2019). However, we still use a probabilistic framework and indicate that various non-trainable image transformations (not only multiple scales) could be of great potential.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Review

This paper proposes "self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders" (selfVAE), which augment the standard VAE framework with discrete, deterministic transformations of the input (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) as auxiliary latent variables, enabling both conditional and unconditional generation with potential applications in compression and super-resolution. While the motivation is reasonable and the idea of leveraging cheap, hand-crafted transformations to decompose a hard generative modeling problem into easier conditional subproblems is intuitively appealing, the paper suffers from several significant issues. First, the novelty is somewhat limited: conditioning VAEs on deterministic downsampled versions of the input closely resembles existing hierarchical/multi-scale approaches (e.g., subscale pixel networks, SR-based generative models, and hierarchical VAEs like NVAE/BIVA), yet the paper does not clearly differentiate itself from these works or provide a careful conceptual comparison. Second, the framing as "self-supervised" is questionable—the transformations used are fixed, deterministic, and hand-designed rather than learned pretext tasks, so the terminology may mislead readers familiar with the self-supervised learning literature. Third, the abstract and introduction are imprecise: claims about "simplifying the objective function" and improved quantitative performance are asserted without adequate technical preview, and the abstract appears to be missing its opening sentence. Clarity is further hurt by dense notation, an abrupt transition into the method section, and an over-reliance on background material (Sections 2.1–2.2) that could be condensed to make room for a sharper articulation of the contribution. Finally, since the central promise involves improved density estimation and compression trade-offs, the evaluation's credibility will hinge heavily on strong baselines (hierarchical VAEs, flow models, neural compression methods) on more than just CIFAR10/Imagenette64/CelebA—reviewers will expect rigorous ablations isolating the contribution of the self-supervised variables from that of the bijective prior, which the paper itself acknowledges plays an important role.

comments.title: Joint factorization p(x|y)p(y) treats y as having nontrivial density despite deterministic definition
comments.quote: However, since we are interested in decomposing the problem of modeling a complex distribution _p_ ( **x** ), we propose to model _p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** ) _p_ ( **y** ) instead
comments.explanation: The authors have just stated p(y|x) = δ(y − d(x)), which means y is a deterministic function of x. This makes p(x,y) = δ(y−d(x))p(x), and marginalizing gives p(y) as a singular distribution supported on the image of d. Modeling p(x|y)p(y) with a continuous/discrete model p_θ(x|y,z) and a learned p(y|u) is therefore not quite modeling the true p(x,y), but rather a surrogate joint where y is treated as a genuine random variable with its own density. The paper never clearly justifies why this surrogate gives a valid bound on log p(x) (the quantity of ultimate interest for density estimation / bpd comparison). This matters for the bpd numbers in Table 1: if the reported bound is on log p(x,y) rather than log p(x), then since y = d(x) is deterministic given x, log p(x,y) = log p(x) + log p(y|x), and the δ contribution is ill-defined for discrete-but-treated-as-continuous y. The paper would benefit from an explicit statement that because y = d(x) deterministically, log p(x,y) = log p(x) in the discrete case (so the bound is on log p(x)), or clarification of how bpd is normalized.

comments.title: Variational factorization q(u,z|x,y) = q(u|y)q(z|x) imposes conditional independence without justification
comments.quote: utilize the variational inference of the form _Q_ ( **u** _,_ **z** _|_ **x** _,_ **y** ) = _q_ ( **u** _|_ **y** ) _q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** )
comments.explanation: The authors propose a mean-field-like factorization where u depends only on y and z depends only on x. Given the generative model where z depends on both u and y (p(z|u,y) appears in the ELBO), the 'ideal' posterior q(z|x,y) would generally depend on y as well, and u's posterior given (x,y) would generally depend on x too. The chosen factorization is a modeling simplification, but no justification is given for why dropping these dependencies is reasonable, especially since the motivation (u captures global, z captures residual detail) suggests z should plausibly be informed by y.

comments.title: Kronecker delta used for a transformation whose output may be continuous
comments.quote: _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) = _δ_ ( **y** _−d_ ( **x** )) due to the deterministic transformation _d_ ( _·_ ), where _δ_ ( _·_ ) is the Kronecker delta
comments.explanation: The Kronecker delta is defined for discrete arguments. For downscaled images with integer pixel values this is consistent with the stated domain X ⊆ Z, so calling it Kronecker is fine. However, transformations like bicubic interpolation (Figure 2.b–d) generally produce non-integer outputs unless explicitly quantized. The authors earlier say the transformations are 'deterministic and discrete,' which suggests they quantize, but this is not explicitly stated — e.g., whether bicubic-downscaled y values are rounded back to 0–255 integers. This is relevant to whether the joint model formulation is well-defined.

comments.title: Missing objective equation for hierarchical selfVAE
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: The text introduces the hierarchical K-level selfVAE and states 'that yields the following objective:' but no equation follows in the visible text (it jumps directly to Section 4 EXPERIMENTS). This looks like the hierarchical ELBO equation (the natural generalization of Eq. 4) should appear here. The variational distribution family introduced just before ('we take K self-supervised data transformations...and the following variational distributions:') similarly has no visible formula. This is a key derivation/definition step for the hierarchical model that is central to the experimental results (3-level selfVAE-downscale on CelebA and Imagenette64).

comments.title: selfVAE-downscale bpd is worse than VAE but claimed as contribution
comments.quote: The selfVAE-downscale achieves worse _bpd_ than the VAE.
comments.explanation: The contribution claim in the introduction states selfVAE 'demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation...on image benchmarks.' On CIFAR-10, Table 1 shows selfVAE-downscale at 3.65 bpd vs VAE (ours) at 3.51 bpd — the selfVAE is worse in density estimation on this benchmark. The improvement claim holds only for Imagenette64 and CelebA (with 3-level hierarchical variant). A careful reader comparing the intro claim to the CIFAR-10 result may find the 'improved quantitative performance...on image benchmarks' somewhat overstated, since density estimation is worse on one of the three benchmarks.

comments.title: Overreaching FID comparison claim
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: Per Table 1: selfVAE-downscale FID is 34.71 (test) / 29.95 (train). DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) is listed at FID 5.24 (3.17*) — dramatically better than selfVAE-downscale. So selfVAE-downscale is NOT better than 'the other generative models'; it is worse than DDPM by a large margin on FID. The claim should be restricted to comparison against the listed flow/VAE baselines with reported FID (PixelCNN 65.93, GLOW 65.93, ResidualFlow 46.37, VAE (ours) 41.36). This is a real overclaim that contradicts the authors' own table.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially useful direction—augmenting VAEs with deterministic image transformations such as downscaling or edge maps to decompose generation into conditional subproblems, with possible benefits for likelihood modeling, sample quality, and compression. However, from the opening sections, the presentation is not yet sufficiently clear or rigorous for a top-tier venue: the abstract appears corrupted or incomplete, several equations/notations are missing or poorly rendered, and the central probabilistic construction is only vaguely motivated rather than formally specified. The main conceptual concern is novelty and positioning: deterministic multiscale or auxiliary representations have strong connections to hierarchical VAEs, Laplacian pyramid/image-to-image generative models, conditional VAEs, super-resolution models, and compression-oriented learned generative models, but the introduction does not yet make clear what is fundamentally new beyond conditioning a VAE on hand-designed transformations. The paper also needs to clarify how deterministic discrete transformations are incorporated into the generative model and ELBO, how unconditional sampling is performed when such variables are functions of data, and whether the claimed simplification of the objective is theoretically justified or merely architectural. Overall, the idea is plausible and may be empirically valuable, but the current framing overstates the contribution relative to the level of formal explanation provided, and the most significant improvements needed are sharper mathematical definition, stronger comparison to related work, cleaner writing/formatting, and more precise claims about what the method contributes beyond existing hierarchical or conditional generative modeling approaches.

comments.title: Performance and conclusion claims overstate or misstate the mixed evidence
comments.quote: iii) This new type of generative model ... demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation and generative capabilities on image benchmarks. / Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models. / In this way, the expressivity of the model will grow and gradually result into richer, better generations. / The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better bpd scores and better generations and reconstructions.
comments.explanation: These claims share the same underlying problem: the paper’s empirical and conceptual conclusions are broader than what is established. The reported bpd results are mixed: for example, CIFAR-10 selfVAE-downscale is worse than the authors’ VAE in bpd, and CelebA selfVAE-sketch is also worse than the VAE. FID is only reported for CIFAR-10, and the claim that selfVAE-downscale has better FID than other generative models contradicts the table’s DDPM result, which is much better. The hierarchy claim is also not isolated by an ablation, so the results do not establish that hierarchy itself, rather than transformation choice or architecture, causes the improvement. Likewise, deterministic transformations may improve inductive bias or effective modeling in these experiments, but they do not by themselves guarantee greater expressivity or high-quality generations.

comments.title: Deterministic self-supervised representation is described as a latent variable
comments.quote: Overall, we end up with a two-level VAE with three latent variables, where one is a data transformation that can be obtained in a self-supervised fashion.
comments.explanation: The intended generative story is understandable: in unconditional generation, y is sampled between u and x. However, in the formal model used for training, y is a deterministic observed transformation, y = d(x), and the variational distribution is over u and z only, Q(u,z | x,y) = q(u|y)q(z|x). Thus calling y a latent variable can mislead readers into thinking that it is inferred as an unobserved stochastic latent during training, rather than treated as an observed auxiliary representation derived from the data.

comments.title: Claim that the objective function is simplified is not substantiated
comments.quote: This class of models allows performing both conditional and unconditional sampling while simplifying the objective function.
comments.explanation: The conditional and unconditional sampling claim is consistent with the later model description, but the claim that the objective function is simplified is unclear. The selfVAE objective introduces additional variables and terms, including p(x|y,z), p(z|u,y), p(y|u), p(u), q(z|x), and q(u|y), and the hierarchical version adds further levels. The method may decompose the modeling task into easier conditional subproblems, but the ELBO is not simpler in the ordinary mathematical sense than a standard VAE objective.

comments.title: Loose use of “tighter lower bound” for changes to prior and decoder
comments.quote: In general, one can obtain a tighter lower bound, and, thus, a more powerful and flexible model, by advancing over the following three components: the encoder ..., the prior ... and the decoder ...
comments.explanation: For a fixed generative model, ELBO tightness relative to log pθ(x) is controlled by the variational posterior, since the gap is KL(qφ(z|x) || pθ(z|x)). Changing the prior or decoder changes the generative model and may improve likelihood or the achieved ELBO, but it is not usually described as making the same lower bound tighter. The sentence blurs the distinction between improving the variational approximation and changing the model class.

comments.title: Discrete-support assumption is unclear for downscaling transformations
comments.quote: In our model, we consider representations that result from deterministic and discrete transformations of an image. Formally, we introduce a transformation d : X[D] ! X[C] that takes x and returns an image representation y, e.g., a downscaled image. ... where p(y | x) = δ(y − d(x)) due to the deterministic transformation d(·), where δ(·) is the Kronecker delta.
comments.explanation: The deterministic construction is clear, but the discreteness assumption is underspecified for downscaling, especially bicubic interpolation. Bicubic downscaling typically produces real-valued intensities unless the output is explicitly rounded or quantized. In that case, a Dirac delta rather than a Kronecker delta would be appropriate, and the use of a discretized logistic likelihood for y would require a precise discretization convention. The paper mentions pixel values between 0 and 255 but also says images are normalized to (0,1), so it is unclear whether transformed images are re-quantized after interpolation.

comments.title: Objective and reported bpd are ambiguous between joint likelihood and marginal image likelihood
comments.quote: However, since we are interested in decomposing the problem of modeling a complex distribution p(x), we propose to model p(x | y)p(y) instead ... ln p(x,y) ≥ ... / Evaluation: We approximate the negative log-likelihood using 512 IW-samples ... and express the scores in bits per dimension (bpd).
comments.explanation: The ELBO is for the augmented joint likelihood p(x,y), with y = d(x) observed during training, not directly for the marginal image likelihood p(x). For discrete y, p(x,d(x)) is one term in the marginal sum p(x)=Σy p(x,y), but the learned model does not appear to enforce support only on pairs satisfying y=d(x). Therefore an IW estimate of p(x,d(x)) is not the same as the standard image likelihood used for VAE, flow, or autoregressive bpd comparisons. If the table reports marginal p(x), the paper should explain how the high-dimensional sum or integration over y is performed; if it reports the joint likelihood, the comparison and normalization need to be stated carefully.

comments.title: Conditional generation equation appears to infer z from the target image rather than sample it conditionally
comments.quote: However, we can use the ground-truth y (i.e, y* = d(x*)), and sample or infer z. Then, the generative process for the former (conditional generation) is: z ⇠ q(z | x*) -> x ⇠ p(x | y*, z).
comments.explanation: The stated equation for conditional generation samples z from q(z|x*), which requires access to the original target image x*. That is posterior inference or reconstruction, not generation from y* alone. Conditional generation from a known representation y* would normally draw z from a conditional prior, for example via p(z|u,y*) with u sampled or inferred from y*. If the authors instead mean posterior reconstruction after encoding x*, then the label “conditional generation” is misleading.

comments.title: CIFAR-10 split and counts are inconsistent with the standard benchmark
comments.quote: CIFAR-10 The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: CIFAR-10 conventionally has 50,000 training images and 10,000 test images, not 60,000 training plus 10,000 validation. The statement that 15% of the training data is set aside as a test set suggests a nonstandard random split rather than evaluation on the official test set. Since the paper compares bpd and FID to published CIFAR-10 results, which are normally evaluated on the official split, the protocol may make the literature comparisons non-comparable unless clarified.

comments.title: CelebA preprocessing and test split are underspecified for bpd comparison
comments.quote: CelebA The Large-scale CelebFaces Attributes (CelebA) Dataset consists of 202.599 images of celebrities. We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the top left corner of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148. Besides the uniform normalization of the image, no other augmentation was applied.
comments.explanation: The crop description is not enough to make the CelebA likelihood numbers reproducible or directly comparable. The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split, nor whether the 148x148 crop is subsequently resized. Many published CelebA likelihood baselines use specific 64x64 preprocessing, and bpd depends strongly on resolution, crop, resizing, and held-out split. Without these details, the comparison to cited baselines is hard to interpret.

comments.title: Compression claims use percentage of data without specifying a coding scheme or bit cost
comments.quote: In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to z (Figure 11).
comments.explanation: This reads like a compression-rate claim, but the paper does not specify an actual coding scheme. For compression, the relevant quantity is the number of bits after quantization and entropy coding, including the cost of transmitting continuous latents, the self-supervised representation, and any side information, together with a defined reconstruction metric. A dimensional percentage such as 16% is not sufficient to establish a compression result.

comments.title: Contrast with prior compression work as non-probabilistic is misleading
comments.quote: A similar approach with a multi-scale auto-encoder for image compression was proposed, e.g, by Mentzer et al. (2019) or Razavi et al. (2019). However, we still use a probabilistic framework and indicate that various non-trainable image transformations (not only multiple scales) could be of great potential.
comments.explanation: The useful distinction seems to be that selfVAE uses fixed, non-trainable transformations inside a VAE-style likelihood model. However, presenting “we still use a probabilistic framework” as the contrast is misleading: learned image-compression methods typically use probabilistic entropy models, and VQ-VAE-2 is itself a probabilistic generative model with learned priors over discrete latents. The sentence risks mischaracterizing the cited prior work and obscuring the actual novelty.

comments.title: Overstated claim of improved quantitative performance
comments.quote: iii) This new type of generative model ( _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders_ ), which is able to perform both conditional and unconditional sampling, demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation and generative capabilities on image benchmarks.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a high-level summary of the experiments, so I checked whether the later quantitative results support it. The context does show some improvements: the hierarchical downscaling selfVAE improves bpd over the authors' VAE on CelebA and Imagenette64, and the CIFAR-10 selfVAE has better FID than the authors' VAE. However, the claim as written is broader than the evidence. On CIFAR-10, selfVAE-downscale has worse density estimation than the authors' VAE, 3.65 bpd versus 3.51. On CelebA, selfVAE-sketch is also worse than the authors' VAE in bpd, 3.24 versus 3.12. FID is only reported for CIFAR-10, so the statement about improved generative capabilities across image benchmarks is not quantitatively established in the table. A narrower claim such as improved FID on CIFAR-10 and improved bpd for the hierarchical downscaling variant on larger images would be supported.

comments.title: Deterministic self-supervised transformation is described as a latent variable
comments.quote: First, we use a single self-supervised transformation as a latent variable, where a transformation is either downscaling or edge detection.
comments.explanation: I can see the authors' intended generative story: the transformed representation y is sampled in the model and then used to generate x, so it is an intermediate random variable during unconditional generation. But in the later formalization, y is also defined as a deterministic observed quantity, y = d(x), and the variational posterior is over u and z, Q(u,z|x,y)=q(u|y)q(z|x), not over y. In standard VAE terminology, a latent variable is unobserved and inferred; here the transformation is constructed from the data during training and evaluation. Calling the transformation itself a latent variable may mislead readers about whether y is inferred, sampled, or deterministically observed. The paper mostly resolves this later by calling it a self-supervised representation, but this sentence remains technically ambiguous.

comments.title: Claim that the objective function is simplified is not substantiated
comments.quote: This class of models allows performing both conditional and unconditional sampling while simplifying the objective function.
comments.explanation: The conditional/unconditional sampling part is consistent with the later model description. The part I could not reconcile is the claim that the objective function is simplified. In the later method section, the selfVAE objective introduces additional variables and terms, including p(x|y,z), p(z|u,y), p(y|u), p(u), q(z|x), and q(u|y), and the hierarchical version adds further levels. That objective may decompose the modeling problem into easier conditional subproblems, which is a plausible and important motivation, but it is not simpler in the ordinary mathematical sense than the standard VAE ELBO. If the intended meaning is computational or statistical simplification via decomposition, the statement should say that rather than suggesting the ELBO itself is simpler.

comments.title: Loose use of “tighter lower bound” for changes to prior and decoder
comments.quote: In general, one can obtain a tighter lower bound, and, thus, a more powerful and flexible model, by advancing over the following three components: the _encoder_ (Rezende et al., 2014; van den Berg et al., 2018; Hoogeboom et al., 2020; Maaløe et al., 2016), the _prior_ (or _marginal_ over latents) (Chen et al., 2016; Habibian et al., 2019; Lavda et al., 2020; Lin & Clark, 2020; Tomczak & Welling, 2017) and the _decoder_ (Gulrajani et al., 2016).
comments.explanation: The general point is reasonable: improving the encoder, prior, and decoder can improve VAE performance. The technical concern is with the phrase “tighter lower bound.” For a fixed generative model, the tightness of the ELBO relative to log pθ(x) is controlled by the variational posterior qφ(z|x), since the gap is KL(qφ(z|x) || pθ(z|x)). Changing the prior or decoder changes the generative model and can increase the achieved ELBO or likelihood, but it is not usually described as making the same lower bound tighter. This is a localized conceptual imprecision in the introduction rather than a fatal problem, but it could confuse the distinction between improving the variational approximation and changing the model class.

comments.title: Discrete-support assumption is unclear for downscaling transformations
comments.quote: In our model, we consider representations that result from _deterministic_ and _discrete_ transformations of an image. Formally, we introduce a transformation _d_ : _X[D] ! X[C]_ that takes **x** and returns an image representation **y** , e.g., a downscaled image. ... where _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) = _δ_ ( **y** _−d_ ( **x** )) due to the deterministic transformation _d_ ( _·_ ), where _δ_ ( _·_ ) is the Kronecker delta.
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear: the auxiliary representation **y** is a deterministic target derived from **x**, so using a point mass for _p_(**y**|**x**) is natural. However, the paper specifically uses downscaling, including bicubic interpolation, as a main example. Bicubic downscaling typically produces real-valued pixel intensities unless the output is explicitly rounded or quantized. In that case the conditional should be represented with a Dirac delta rather than a Kronecker delta, and the later use of a discretized logistic likelihood for **y** would require a precise discretization convention. The surrounding text partly resolves this by saying images are represented by values between 0 and 255, but the experimental setup also says images are normalized to (0, 1), and it is not stated whether transformed images are re-quantized after interpolation. This matters for the formal likelihood and for reproducibility of the self-supervised targets.

comments.title: Calling the deterministic representation a latent variable conflicts with the formal model
comments.quote: Overall, we end up with a two-level VAE with three latent variables, where one is a data transformation that can be obtained in a self-supervised fashion.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: in the generative story, **y** is sampled between **u** and **x**, so it plays an internal generative role, while at training time it is obtained automatically from **x**. But in the formal model immediately following this passage, **y** is not latent in the usual VAE sense: it is an observed deterministic transformation, **y** = _d_(**x**), and the variational distribution is over **u** and **z** only, _Q_(**u**, **z** | **x**, **y**) = _q_(**u**|**y**) _q_(**z**|**x**). Thus the phrase “three latent variables” can mislead a reader into thinking that **y** is inferred as an unobserved stochastic latent during training, rather than treated as an observed auxiliary variable with a deterministic data-generation rule. A more precise phrasing would distinguish the continuous latent variables **u**, **z** from the deterministic/self-supervised observed representation **y**.

comments.title: The objective is for the augmented joint likelihood, while the prose frames it as modeling p(x)
comments.quote:
However, since we are interested in decomposing the problem of modeling a complex distribution _p_ ( **x** ), we propose to model _p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** ) _p_ ( **y** ) instead, and utilize the variational inference of the form _Q_ ( **u** _,_ **z** _|_ **x** _,_ **y** ) = _q_ ( **u** _|_ **y** ) _q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** ) that yields:

ln _p_ ( **x** _,_ **y** ) _≥_ E _Q_ ⇥ ln _p✓_ ( **x** _|_ **y** _,_ **z** ) + ln _p_ ( **z** _|_ **u** _,_ **y** ) + ln _p_ ( **y** _|_ **u** ) + ln _p_ ( **u** ) _−_ ln _q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** ) _−_ ln _q_ ( **u** _|_ **y** )⇤ _._ (4)
comments.explanation: The ELBO itself is consistent with the generative factorization _p_(**u**) _p_(**y**|**u**) _p_(**z**|**u**,**y**) _p_(**x**|**y**,**z**). What remains somewhat underspecified is the relation between this augmented-data objective and the stated goal of modeling _p_(**x**). Since **y** is deterministically computed from **x** in the data, training on log _p_(**x**, _d_(**x**)) is a reasonable surrogate, and for discrete **y** the joint term gives a lower bound on the marginal log _p_(**x**) because _p_(**x**) sums over possible **y** values. However, this distinction is important: the bound being optimized is explicitly on the joint augmented observation, not directly on the marginal image likelihood unless one marginalizes over **y** or states that the joint term is used as a lower bound to it. This becomes especially relevant when later reporting bits-per-dimension against ordinary VAEs, because the comparison depends on whether the score is the marginal likelihood of **x**, the joint likelihood of (**x**, **y**), or a lower bound using only the deterministic **y** = _d_(**x**) term.

comments.title: Conditional generation equation appears to infer z from the target image rather than sample it conditionally
comments.quote:
However, we can use the ground-truth **y** (i.e, **y** _[⇤]_ = _d_ ( **x** _[⇤]_ )), and sample or infer **z** . Then, the generative process for the former ( _conditional generation_ ) is:

**z** _⇠ q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** _[⇤]_ ) _−!_ **x** _⇠ p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** _[⇤] ,_ **z** ) _,_
comments.explanation: The distinction the authors seem to want is useful: given a self-supervised representation **y**\* such as a low-resolution image, one can either sample missing details or reconstruct details inferred from the original image. But the stated equation for “conditional generation” samples **z** from _q_(**z**|**x**\*), which requires access to the original target image **x**\*. That is inference/reconstruction, not generation from **y**\* alone. If the intended operation is conditional generation from a known **y**\*, one would expect **z** to be drawn from a conditional prior, e.g. involving _p_(**z**|**u**, **y**\*) with **u** sampled or inferred from **y**\*, rather than from an encoder that conditions on the high-resolution image. If the authors instead mean “sample from the approximate posterior after encoding **x**\*,” then the label “conditional generation” and the preceding “sample or infer” distinction are misleading. This ambiguity affects the interpretation of the later reconstruction/compression procedures.

comments.title: Claim that added transformations increase expressivity is stronger than what is established here
comments.quote: In this way, the expressivity of the model will grow and gradually result into richer, better generations.
comments.explanation: The intuition is plausible: decomposing image modeling into a hierarchy over transformed representations can make learning easier and can improve sample quality in practice. However, the statement as written sounds like a guaranteed consequence of introducing deterministic transformations. A deterministic auxiliary representation does not by itself guarantee a strictly more expressive marginal family over **x**; depending on model capacity, factorization choices, and likelihood assumptions, it may mainly change the inductive bias or optimization problem. The later experiments provide empirical support for some datasets, but this motivational passage does not justify the stronger general claim that expressivity “will grow” and generations will become better. A more careful wording would say that the decomposition is expected to improve inductive bias or effective modeling capacity.

comments.title: Reported selfVAE bpd is ambiguous because the model likelihood is over \((\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\), not clearly over \(\mathbf{x}\)
comments.quote: **Evaluation** We approximate the negative log-likelihood using 512 IW-samples (Burda et al., 2015) and express the scores in bits per dimension ( _bpd_ ).
comments.explanation: At first this sounds like the standard generative-model evaluation protocol: estimate \(-\log p(\mathbf{x})\) with importance samples and report bits per image dimension. But earlier in the paper the selfVAE objective is an ELBO for \(\log p(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\), where \(\mathbf{y}=d(\mathbf{x})\) is deterministic in the data but is modeled generatively through \(p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{u})\). I checked whether determinism of \(d\) resolves this: for the empirical distribution, \((\mathbf{x},d(\mathbf{x}))\) contains the same information as \(\mathbf{x}\), but the learned model does not appear to enforce that \(p(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\) has support only on pairs with \(\mathbf{y}=d(\mathbf{x})\). Therefore \(p(\mathbf{x},d(\mathbf{x}))\) is generally a joint likelihood term, not the marginal image likelihood \(p(\mathbf{x})=\sum_{\mathbf{y}}p(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\). If the table reports an IW estimate of \(p(\mathbf{x},d(\mathbf{x}))\), it is not directly the standard bpd used for VAE/flow/autoregressive baselines unless the normalization and coding interpretation are carefully specified. If instead the authors approximate \(p(\mathbf{x})\), the summation/integration over high-dimensional discrete \(\mathbf{y}\) needs to be described. This ambiguity affects the central quantitative comparison.

comments.title: CIFAR-10 split/counts are inconsistent with the standard benchmark and may make literature comparisons non-comparable
comments.quote: _CIFAR-10_ The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: The intent seems to be to describe a train/test protocol before comparing bpd and FID to standard published numbers. However, CIFAR-10 conventionally has 50,000 training images and 10,000 test images, i.e. 60,000 total images, not 60,000 training plus 10,000 validation. I considered whether this could be a minor wording slip, but the next sentence says they set aside 15% of the training data as the test set, suggesting a nonstandard random split rather than the official CIFAR-10 test set. That matters because Table 1 compares against published CIFAR-10 results, which are normally evaluated on the official test set. Even if the difference is not huge statistically, the evaluation protocol is not the same and the comparison should either use the standard test set or explicitly state that the literature numbers are not directly comparable.

comments.title: FID claims overlook the much better DDPM result shown in the same table
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that selfVAE-downscale improves over their own VAE in FID, and it is also better than several older baselines listed in the table. But I checked the table itself: DDPM is reported with FID 5.24, and 3.17 on the training set, whereas selfVAE-downscale has FID 34.71, or 29.95 on the training set. Thus the statement that selfVAE-downscale has significantly better FID than the other generative models is not correct as written. The claim would be defensible if restricted to their VAE baseline or to the subset of non-diffusion baselines shown, but in its current form it contradicts the reported numbers.

comments.title: CelebA preprocessing and test split are underspecified for a bpd comparison
comments.quote: _CelebA_ The Large-scale CelebFaces Attributes (CelebA) Dataset consists of 202 _._ 599 images of celebrities. We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the top left corner of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148. Besides the uniform normalization of the image, no other augmentation was applied.
comments.explanation: The crop description gives part of the preprocessing, which is helpful, but for likelihood comparisons on CelebA the exact preprocessing and split are crucial. I looked for whether the passage specifies a train/validation/test split analogous to CIFAR-10 and Imagenette64, but it does not. It also says images are cropped to 148 by 148, but does not state whether they are subsequently resized; many published CelebA likelihood baselines, including RealNVP-style comparisons, use specific 64x64 preprocessing. Since bpd depends on image resolution, crop, resizing, and held-out split, the CelebA numbers in Table 1 are hard to reproduce and may not be directly comparable to the cited RealNVP baseline unless the appendix fully pins these choices down.

comments.title: Compression claims use percentage of data without specifying an actual coding scheme or bit cost
comments.quote: In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to **z** (Figure 11).
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is plausible: a deterministic sketch plus latent variables can trade off reconstruction quality against the amount of side information. However, the phrase “using only 16% of the original data” reads like a compression-rate claim. For a probabilistic compression method, the relevant quantity is not just the dimensionality or number of stored variables, but the number of bits after quantization and entropy coding, including the cost of transmitting continuous latents, any discrete self-supervised representation, model assumptions shared by encoder/decoder, and reconstruction fidelity metrics. The passage does not specify such a coding scheme, and the evidence cited appears qualitative. Thus the compression conclusion is suggestive, but the stated percentage and “high accuracy” claim are not sufficiently justified as an actual compression result.

comments.title: Overgeneralized claim that deterministic transformations yield high-quality generations
comments.quote: In this paper, we showed that taking deterministic and discrete transformations results in coherent generations of high visual quality, and allows to integrate prior knowledge without loosing its unconditional generative functionality.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended summary: the model construction does preserve unconditional sampling through the learned chain \(u \to y \to z \to x\), and the qualitative figures suggest that some self-supervised transformations can improve visual coherence. That part is consistent with the earlier generative process. What remains problematic is the stronger wording that deterministic and discrete transformations as such “result” in coherent, high-quality generations. The empirical support is mixed: on CIFAR-10, selfVAE-downscale has worse bpd than the authors’ VAE, though better FID; for CelebA and Imagenette64, no FID or other quantitative sample-quality metric is reported; and only downscaling/sketching are actually tested. Thus the sentence reads like a general causal conclusion about deterministic transformations, whereas the evidence supports a narrower claim that the proposed transformations can improve qualitative coherence in some settings.

comments.title: Broad claim that hierarchical architectures perform better is only partially supported
comments.quote: The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better _bpd_ scores and better generations and reconstructions.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as referring specifically to the hierarchical downscale selfVAE, and in that narrower sense the table does show improved bpd over the authors’ VAE on CelebA and Imagenette64. The wording “seem to confirm” is also appropriately cautious. However, the claim is broader than the presented evidence. The paper does not appear to provide an ablation isolating hierarchy from the particular transformation choice, architecture, or dataset size, and the CIFAR-10 result for the non-hierarchical selfVAE shows worse bpd than the VAE. The claims about “better generations and reconstructions” are mostly supported by qualitative figures rather than systematic quantitative reconstruction or perceptual metrics. A more precise conclusion would restrict the claim to the tested hierarchical downscaling models and distinguish bpd evidence from qualitative visual evidence.

comments.title: Contrast with prior compression work as non-probabilistic is misleading
comments.quote: A similar approach with a multi-scale auto-encoder for image compression was proposed, e.g, by Mentzer et al. (2019) or Razavi et al. (2019). However, we still use a probabilistic framework and indicate that various non-trainable image transformations (not only multiple scales) could be of great potential.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended distinction seems to be that selfVAE uses fixed, non-trainable transformations within a VAE-style likelihood model, whereas the cited works use learned multi-scale representations. That is a useful distinction. But the sentence frames “we still use a probabilistic framework” as the contrast with Mentzer et al. and Razavi et al., which is not quite accurate. Learned image-compression methods typically rely on probabilistic entropy models, and VQ-VAE-2 is itself a probabilistic generative model with learned priors over discrete latents. Also, Razavi et al. (2019) is primarily a hierarchical generative model rather than an image-compression paper in the same sense as Mentzer et al. The remaining issue is not the comparison itself, but that the stated contrast may mischaracterize the cited prior work and obscure the real novelty: deterministic/non-trainable self-supervised transformations inside a VAE framework.
